text.title: Strangler-Fig Migration Playbook (Vendor Delivery) Decision Tree
text.summary: Routing tree from observable signal to a rule id in `01-core-rules.xml`. Every leaf resolves to either a rule id or `skip-this-methodology`.

decision-tree.root-question: Which observable signal best describes the current state of the artefact under review?

conclusion.verdict: nothing to strangle; pick microservices-architecture instead

branch.question: Is the monolith <30 routes and <50 KLOC?

conclusion.verdict: big-bang rewrite is safer for very small monoliths

branch.question: Did the team select a slice with coupling_depth ≥4 first?

conclusion.verdict: drop it; pick low-coupling rails-first slices to validate the platform

branch.question: Is the planned burn-in <168 hours (7 days)?

conclusion.verdict: extend to ≥7 days at 100% traffic with ±10% parity gates

branch.question: Is the kill-switch revert path untested before cutover?

conclusion.verdict: test end-to-end in staging (or percentage rollback in prod) within 7 days of cutover

branch.question: Is dual-write configured without an explicit termination date?

conclusion.verdict: add termination date; switch to shadow-read after burn-in

branch.question: Is the SOW missing per-slice exit criteria?

conclusion.verdict: add the three exit-criteria gates (legacy 5xx, parity, runbook handoff) to the contract
signal does not match; route to a different methodology or proceed without this one

section.title: Usage
section.p: Apply the tree top-to-bottom against the artefact (PR diff, config, design doc). The first matching branch is decisive; later branches are not evaluated. If multiple branches match, fix in the order listed — they typically depend on earlier fixes.
